title: The Broken Promise
bullets: The Fourteenth and Fifteenth Amendments of 1868 and 1870 made Black Americans citizens with the vote, then the federal government withdrew its troops in 1877 and stopped enforcing them.
The white South built Jim Crow on three pillars: segregation by law, disfranchisement through poll taxes, literacy tests, grandfather clauses, and the all-white primary, and terror by lynching.
Plessy v. Ferguson in 1896 ruled separate but equal facilities constitutional, and the doctrine stood fifty-eight years while the Southern filibuster killed every anti-lynching bill.
The Great Depression hit Black workers first and hardest, and Roosevelt's New Deal helped unevenly because Southern Democrats demanded farm and domestic workers be excluded.
A second Great Migration carried roughly five million Black Southerners north and west between 1940 and 1970 into cities the movement would later mobilize.
A. Philip Randolph's threatened 1941 march forced Roosevelt to create the Fair Employment Practice Committee, proving credible mass disruption could extract federal concessions.
Black soldiers fought a segregated war under the Double-V campaign, the Tuskegee Airmen demolished the aptitude lie, and Truman desegregated the military in 1948.
Charles Hamilton Houston and Thurgood Marshall built a decades-long NAACP legal campaign, winning Gaines, Sweatt, and McLaurin and arming Brown with the Clark doll studies.

title: The Earthquake
bullets: On May 17, 1954, a unanimous Supreme Court under Chief Justice Earl Warren decided Brown v. Board of Education, holding segregated schools inherently unequal and overturning Plessy.
Warren engineered the 9–0 ruling deliberately, knowing a divided Court would invite defiance on a question this charged.
Brown was also a Cold War event: Soviet propaganda used American segregation against a decolonizing world the United States was courting.
Brown II in 1955 declined to set a deadline and told the South to desegregate with all deliberate speed, which segregationists correctly read as permission to delay forever.
In 1956, 101 of 128 Southern members of Congress signed the Southern Manifesto, launching Massive Resistance and reviving the old interposition doctrine.
Prince Edward County, Virginia shut its entire public school system for five years rather than admit Black children, funneling public money to white private academies.
The White Citizens' Councils used economic destruction, circulating the names of Black petition-signers to employers and banks until signing meant losing your job and credit.
Ten years after Brown, in 1964, well under two percent of Black children in the Deep South attended an integrated school.

title: Mississippi, 1955
bullets: In August 1955 fourteen-year-old Emmett Till, visiting from Chicago, was abducted, tortured, shot, and thrown in the Tallahatchie River weighted with a cotton-gin fan.
His mother Mamie Till insisted on an open casket so the world could see what they did to her baby, and tens of thousands filed past it in Chicago.
The Black press, especially Jet magazine, the Chicago Defender, and the Pittsburgh Courier, carried the photographs nationwide as the movement's nervous system.
An all-white, all-male jury acquitted the two killers in about an hour, with one juror joking the deliberation would have been shorter without a soft-drink break.
Protected by double jeopardy, the two men sold a full, detailed confession to Look magazine the next year for a few thousand dollars.
No federal murder statute reached a private killing in 1955, so Mississippi's one-hour acquittal was the law's final word.
A strikingly large number of the movement's future organizers later said the Till photograph was the moment they decided they would no longer accept this.

title: 381 Days
bullets: Rosa Parks was not a tired seamstress but the longtime Montgomery NAACP secretary trained at the Highlander Folk School, and her December 1, 1955 refusal was disciplined courage.
Nine months earlier fifteen-year-old Claudette Colvin had done the identical act, but leaders judged she could not withstand the character assassination segregationists would unleash.
Jo Ann Robinson and the Women's Political Council mimeographed tens of thousands of leaflets overnight, so the boycott began with women running a printing press.
The community formed the Montgomery Improvement Association and chose twenty-six-year-old Martin Luther King Jr. as president largely because he was new in town and had no enemies.
The boycott lasted 381 days, and the MIA built a private transportation system of volunteer cars, Black-owned taxis, and dispatch points coordinated through the churches.
The Black church supplied the buildings, money, leadership, mass-meeting culture, and music that held morale through arrests and a bomb thrown at King's house.
The boycott won two ways at once: it economically strangled the bus company, and the NAACP's Browder v. Gayle suit struck bus segregation down at the Supreme Court in November 1956.

title: Building the Machine
bullets: In 1957 King and a group of mostly Baptist ministers founded the Southern Christian Leadership Conference to run the Montgomery model through the network of Black churches.
Ella Baker distrusted the charismatic-leader model, insisting strong people don't need strong leaders and that the movement made Martin, not Martin the movement.
Bayard Rustin, a Quaker pacifist and superb logistician, had studied Gandhian nonviolence most deeply and taught it to King, but was kept in the background as openly gay with a Communist past.
In September 1957 Governor Orval Faubus used the Arkansas National Guard to block the Little Rock Nine, a performance that secured his white base and won him repeated re-election.
President Eisenhower federalized the Guard and sent the 101st Airborne, the first federal troops sent south to protect Black rights since Reconstruction.
Cooper v. Aaron in 1958 unanimously declared no state official could nullify a federal constitutional ruling.
Senate Majority Leader Lyndon Johnson gutted the Civil Rights Act of 1957 procedurally, stripping Title III and adding a jury-trial requirement that made it nearly unusable.
Strom Thurmond held the floor against the gutted bill for over twenty-four hours, the longest solo filibuster in Senate history, though his own Southern colleagues were furious.

title: The Students Sit Down
bullets: On February 1, 1960, four Black freshmen sat down at the whites-only Woolworth's lunch counter in Greensboro, North Carolina, and within two months the sit-in spread to dozens of cities.
The sit-in required no national organization, no money, and no permission, just disciplined people willing to be hurt, and put the cruelty of segregation in an undeniable setting.
CORE, founded in 1942, had run sit-ins in the 1940s, but they did not catch fire before Brown, Montgomery, and the Black press built a connected, expectant Black public.
The Nashville movement, including Diane Nash and John Lewis, was drilled for months by James Lawson in the theology and technique of nonviolence.
Nonviolence was a calculated method for transferring moral authority by provoking the oppressor into revealing, before witnesses, what the system actually was.
Ella Baker used SCLC money to convene the students in April 1960 and argued they should form their own independent, decentralized organization rather than become a youth arm.
They founded the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, SNCC, the era's boldest organization, deliberately built to not have a charismatic leader.

title: Riding Into the Fire
bullets: In May 1961 an interracial group of thirteen Freedom Riders boarded two buses in Washington intending to be attacked on purpose so the government would enforce the law.
Boynton v. Virginia in 1960 had ruled segregation in interstate bus terminals illegal, but the Deep South still rigidly segregated them and Washington did nothing.
Outside Anniston, Alabama, a mob firebombed one bus and beat the riders as they escaped, and the photograph of the burning bus went around the world.
Diane Nash and the Nashville students refused to let the rides end, arguing that letting violence stop the movement once would teach every segregationist that enough violence works.
John and Robert Kennedy treated civil rights as a political problem and Cold War embarrassment, seeking a cooling-off period and the minimum that would restore order.
Under pressure from Robert Kennedy, the Interstate Commerce Commission issued binding regulations in late 1961 that finally desegregated the terminals.
In Albany, Georgia, the SCLC failed because police chief Laurie Pritchett made calm mass arrests without visible violence, denying the movement its televised brutality.
Albany taught the movement that the opponent was a variable: you chose a target where the man in charge would supply the violence the strategy required.

title: Birmingham
bullets: In 1963 the SCLC chose Birmingham, the most rigidly segregated big city in America, nicknamed Bombingham, because they could count on commissioner Bull Connor.
Project C, the C for confrontation, planned to fill the jails, boycott downtown stores at Easter, and provoke a response ugly enough to force the Kennedy administration off the sidelines.
The Children's Crusade, proposed by James Bevel, sent schoolchildren as young as six to march and be arrested when adult volunteers ran short.
In May 1963 Bull Connor turned high-pressure fire hoses and police attack dogs on children, and it ran on the national news and front pages worldwide.
Television brought into white living rooms what segregation looked like when defended, the largest third party in human history watching the provoked confrontation.
King, held in solitary, wrote the Letter from Birmingham Jail answering the white moderate who preferred order to justice and counseled patience.
The autumn before, James Meredith won a court order admitting him to Ole Miss, Governor Ross Barnett defied it, and a deadly mob siege forced the Army in.
Birmingham pushed President Kennedy to frame civil rights as a moral issue in a June 1963 televised address and propose the bill that became the Civil Rights Act of 1964.

title: The Summit and the Bomb
bullets: On August 28, 1963, roughly 250,000 people gathered at the Lincoln Memorial for the March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom, the realization of Randolph's threatened 1941 march.
Randolph insisted on Jobs beside Freedom because legal desegregation without economic justice would free Black Americans into poverty, the seed of the Poor People's Campaign.
Bayard Rustin built the logistics of moving and feeding a quarter-million people in a day, the brilliant organizer the movement still would not let stand in front.
The Big Six led the march, and John Lewis was forced to soften his SNCC speech criticizing the Kennedy bill minutes before he spoke.
Not one woman who built the movement, not Baker, Hamer, Nash, or Dorothy Height, addressed the march.
King's I Have a Dream closing was substantially improvised after Mahalia Jackson called out, Tell them about the dream, Martin.
Two months before the march Medgar Evers was shot in his Mississippi driveway, and his killer went free for three decades until a 1994 conviction.
Eighteen days after the march, Klansmen bombed the 16th Street Baptist Church in Birmingham, killing four girls, and in November Kennedy was assassinated in Dallas.

title: The Law
bullets: Lyndon Johnson, the Texan who as Majority Leader had gutted the 1957 act, decided to pass the stalled civil rights bill in strong form as a memorial to Kennedy.
The obstacle was the filibuster: Senate debate was unlimited, and only cloture, then requiring a two-thirds supermajority, could force an end.
Richard Russell divided eighteen Southern Democrats into three platoons of six to talk the bill to death in a disciplined relay.
The filibuster ran for fifty-seven days on the bill's substance, the longest filibuster on a civil rights measure in Senate history.
Passage was bipartisan by necessity, and Republican leader Everett Dirksen delivered the votes after extracting changes narrowing federal enforcement.
On June 10, 1964, cloture passed seventy-one to twenty-nine, the first time the Senate ever invoked it on a civil rights bill, with a dying Clair Engle pointing to his eye.
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 outlawed segregation in public accommodations and banned discrimination by race, color, religion, sex, or national origin in employment.
Congress grounded the act in the Commerce Clause rather than the Fourteenth Amendment, and the Supreme Court upheld it unanimously in Heart of Atlanta Motel.
The act destroyed de jure segregation but did very little about de facto segregation produced by where banks lent and highways ran, the hinge of the rest of the era.

title: Mississippi Burning
bullets: The 1964 act had not touched the vote, and in Mississippi almost no Black citizen could cast a ballot.
SNCC organizers led by Bob Moses had lived for years in the most dangerous rural counties, working voter registration with the older NAACP network of Medgar Evers and Amzie Moore.
Freedom Summer brought roughly a thousand mostly white Northern college students into Mississippi to register voters and run Freedom Schools.
Organizers recruited white students explicitly because the country would not ignore violence done to white students from prominent Northern families.
James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner were murdered at the start of the summer and buried in an earthen dam, found six weeks later.
Black Mississippians built the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and went to Atlantic City to be seated in place of the all-white state delegation.
Fannie Lou Hamer testified on national television until President Johnson called an impromptu press conference to knock her off the broadcasts.
Johnson dictated a compromise of two non-voting at-large seats delivered by Humphrey, Reuther, and Mondale, and the MFDP rejected it, breaking SNCC's faith in the liberal coalition.

title: The Bridge
bullets: Selma was chosen the way Birmingham had been, a county where Black citizens were a majority and a tiny fraction of registered voters, with a violent sheriff in Jim Clark.
In February 1965 a state trooper killed church deacon Jimmie Lee Jackson, and organizers including James Bevel proposed a fifty-four-mile march to Montgomery.
On March 7, 1965, state troopers and a mounted posse attacked about six hundred marchers at the Edmund Pettus Bridge, fracturing John Lewis's skull on Bloody Sunday.
The bridge was named for Edmund Pettus, a Confederate general and Reconstruction-era Klan grand dragon then elected to the United States Senate.
Two days later King led marchers to the bridge, had them kneel and pray, and turned them around on Turnaround Tuesday rather than defy a federal injunction.
Viola Liuzzo, a white Detroit mother of five ferrying marchers, was shot dead by Klansmen, and one of the four men in the car was a paid FBI informant.
Johnson addressed Congress, called Black disfranchisement a moral failure, and ended by speaking the movement's anthem, And we shall overcome.
The Voting Rights Act of 1965 suspended literacy tests, sent federal examiners to register voters, and imposed preclearance, finally giving the Fifteenth Amendment teeth ninety-five years late.
In Mississippi the share of voting-age Black citizens on the rolls climbed from roughly five to seven percent past sixty percent within a few years.

title: The Other America
bullets: Five days after Johnson signed the Voting Rights Act, the Watts section of Los Angeles erupted into six days of fire that left thirty-four people dead in August 1965.
Watts had no whites-only signs to defy and no Bull Connor to provoke, exposing the limit of the Southern strategy against de facto segregation.
Redlining had federal housing agencies mark Black neighborhoods in red as hazardous, routing the postwar homeownership boom to white families and structurally closed to Black ones.
Urban renewal and highway construction bulldozed functioning Black neighborhoods, what James Baldwin called Negro removal.
King brought the SCLC north to the Chicago Freedom Movement in 1966, where Marquette Park mobs threw bricks and he said he had never seen hatred worse than in Chicago.
The Chicago campaign's one durable creation was economic: Operation Breadbasket, run by a young Jesse Jackson, applied the boycott weapon to Northern businesses.
Johnson's Great Society and War on Poverty delivered Medicare, Medicaid, Head Start, and food stamps, cutting the national poverty rate by roughly half over the decade.
The Economic Opportunity Act's maximum feasible participation clause gave the poor leverage, and city halls fought bitterly to strangle the programs.

title: The Longer Tradition
bullets: A second tradition of Black self-defense, self-determination, and separatism ran continuously alongside the nonviolent movement and did not appear in 1966.
Malcolm X joined the Nation of Islam, a heterodox movement led by Elijah Muhammad teaching Black separatism and the doctrine that the white man would never grant Black people anything.
Malcolm argued that asking your oppressor for freedom while promising not to defend yourself was a strategy of humiliation, mocking the March on Washington as the Farce on Washington.
Malcolm left the Nation in 1964, made the hajj, embraced Sunni Islam, and was assassinated by Nation gunmen in a Harlem ballroom in February 1965, three weeks before Bloody Sunday.
The Autobiography of Malcolm X, written with Alex Haley and published months after his death, became the foundational text of what came next.
Robert F. Williams armed his Monroe, North Carolina NAACP chapter, drove off a Klan motorcade in 1957, was suspended by the national NAACP, and fled into exile.
His 1962 book Negroes With Guns directly influenced Huey Newton and the Black Panther Party.
The Deacons for Defense and Justice, armed Black veterans in Louisiana, were frequently the unspoken reason a nonviolent campaign could survive in a Klan county.

title: Black Power
bullets: In June 1966 James Meredith was shot from ambush walking across Mississippi, and on the continued March Against Fear SNCC's Stokely Carmichael led a chant of Black Power.
To Carmichael and SNCC, Black Power meant self-determination: Black people controlling their own communities, institutions, economics, and political organizations.
SNCC had built a working model in Lowndes County, Alabama, an independent Black party whose ballot symbol was a black panther.
SNCC and later CORE voted in 1966 and 1967 to become Black-led and Black-staffed, ending the interracial-coalition model.
In Oakland in October 1966 Huey Newton and Bobby Seale founded the Black Panther Party, pairing armed copwatching with a Ten-Point Program and survival programs.
A 1969 FBI memo identified the Free Breakfast for Children program, not the guns, as the greatest single threat in need of disruption.
COINTELPRO, the FBI's illegal Counter Intelligence Program later exposed by the Church Committee, infiltrated organizations and worked to neutralize Black leaders.
The FBI mailed King an anonymous package and letter calling him an evil, abnormal beast and urging him to kill himself before the Nobel Peace Prize.
In December 1969 Chicago police raided Fred Hampton's apartment on an FBI informant's floor plan and shot the twenty-one-year-old Panther leader in his bed.

title: The Fire This Time
bullets: The summers of 1965 through 1967 brought disorders to more than a hundred cities, the largest being Newark with 26 dead and Detroit with 43 dead and federal paratroopers deployed.
The Kerner Commission concluded in 1968 the country was moving toward two societies, one black, one white, separate and unequal, with white racism the fundamental cause.
On April 4, 1967, King delivered Beyond Vietnam at Riverside Church, naming his government the greatest purveyor of violence in the world and linking racism, militarism, and economic exploitation.
Muhammad Ali refused induction in 1967 on religious and political grounds and was stripped of his title, convicted, and barred from boxing for three prime years.
Beyond Vietnam was condemned across the establishment that had praised the 1963 dream, and King's public approval fell sharply.
King's Poor People's Campaign aimed to bring a multiracial army of the poor to Washington to demand an economic bill of rights.
He went to Memphis to support striking Black sanitation workers after Echol Cole and Robert Walker were crushed to death in a packer truck, marching under signs reading I Am a Man.
On April 4, 1968, King was shot dead on the balcony of the Lorraine Motel at thirty-nine, setting off the Holy Week uprising in more than a hundred cities.
Congress passed the Fair Housing Act within a week, Resurrection City rose and was bulldozed in the mud, and Robert Kennedy was assassinated two months later.

title: Backlash
bullets: Governor George Wallace pledged segregation forever, stood in a University of Alabama doorway in 1963, and drew large Northern white working-class votes running for president in 1964, 1968, and 1972.
In 1968 Richard Nixon's Southern Strategy courted disaffected white voters through coded appeals to law and order, forced busing, and states' rights.
The realignment inverted the Reconstruction configuration almost term for term: the white Southern Democratic bloc became Republican and Black voters became overwhelmingly Democratic.
Green in 1968 ruled freedom-of-choice plans insufficient and imposed an affirmative duty to desegregate root and branch, finally supplying the deadline Brown II withheld.
Swann in 1971 unanimously upheld court-ordered busing, which became the most explosive racial issue of the 1970s, North as much as South.
When Boston was ordered to bus students between South Boston and Roxbury in 1974, mobs of several thousand stoned the buses carrying Black children for years.
Milliken v. Bradley in 1974 ruled 5–4 that busing could not cross the city-suburb line, walling off white suburbs and capping school desegregation.
Griggs v. Duke Power in 1971 established disparate impact, and Nixon's 1969 Philadelphia Plan imposed minority-hiring goals as both a civil rights goal and a wedge against Democrats.
Shirley Chisholm ran for president in 1972 unbought and unbossed, the Congressional Black Caucus organized in 1971, and roughly five hundred Black officials became several thousand.

title: The Second Reconstruction
bullets: In 1868 and 1870 the Constitution promised Black Americans citizenship, equal protection, and the vote, and the first Reconstruction did not fail but was destroyed on purpose by 1877.
Between 1954 and 1965 a mass movement forced the country to enforce the amendments: Brown killed Plessy, the 1964 act destroyed legal segregation, the 1965 act gave the Fifteenth Amendment teeth.
Unlike after the first Reconstruction, the restored Black franchise survived the backlash, converting within a generation into thousands of Black elected officials.
Loving v. Virginia in 1967 struck down every remaining law criminalizing interracial marriage in the case of Richard and Mildred Loving.
The movement became the template for the Chicano, Native American, women's, disability, and gay-liberation movements and carried abroad to Northern Ireland and South Africa.
The structural segregation of the Northern and suburban city survived nearly intact, and the economic agenda of Jobs beside Freedom was never enacted.
The forty acres not given in 1865 and the jobs-and-income guarantee refused in 1968 are the same promise refused twice for the same reason.
The radical, anti-war, anti-capitalist King of 1967 and 1968 was compressed into one sentence from one 1963 speech, a federal holiday selling Congress the safest possible King.
Restoring the texture of the foot soldiers, the women, the organizers, and the unfinished economic demand is not revisionism but the repair.
